result:
There are only two kinds of silence: the one that waits to be broken and the one that has already devoured every possible sound. I’ve been living inside the second kind for so long that I forget it isn’t a room, it’s a stomach. The wallpaper is acid pink with tiny fleurs-de-lis the color of tired teeth. When I press my ear to it I can hear my mother laughing in 1987, the year she still believed the sky was a democracy. She laughed like a bike tire losing air, a soft collapse that sounded like forgiveness. I keep that laugh folded in the matchbox I carry where a heart is supposed to go. Sometimes I strike it just to see the sulfur bloom: a private sunrise for an audience of one.

The city outside has been under renovation since the invention of nostalgia. Cranes swing their necks like brontosauruses in mourning. Every morning a new coffee franchise opens inside the corpse of the previous coffee franchise; baristas in green aprons perform grief choreography, pulling shots of espresso that taste exactly like the moment you realize your father’s jacket will never fit you. I order a “medium” because “large” is too honest. While I wait I watch a man on the sidewalk folding paper cranes from parking tickets. He gifts them to pigeons who accept the weight of the law without complaint. The birds have learned to speak in penalties: fifteen minutes, thirty, an entire afternoon evaporating under the heat lamp of debt.

I used to keep a list of every place I’ve cried. Movie theater, row G, seat 8: opening night of a superhero sequel, the moment the cape caught fire and the audience cheered while I mourned the unseen stuntman. Supermarket, aisle 9, beside the gluten-free breadcrumbs: a song came on that reminded me of a cousin who joined a cult that worships static electricity; she mails me dryer sheets every Christmas. Airport gate B22: the airline announced a delay and the entire waiting room exhaled in unison, a communal surrender so intimate I orgasmed a single tear. After a while the list became indistinguishable from a love letter to the world’s willingness to hurt me. I mailed it to myself without a return address; the post office lost it, or maybe it’s still traveling, an origami passport accumulating exotic postmarks: Griefgrad, Melancholia, the People’s Republic of Almost Okay.

My upstairs neighbor is teaching herself the violin by playing only the notes that scare the ghosts away. She rehearses at 3 a.m. because that’s when the building’s dead are most argumentative. Through the ceiling I hear her negotiate with a Victorian child who insists on leaving handprints in the butter. She promises to flatten the curve of every B-flat until the girl agrees to evaporate. Progress is slow. Last week a poltergeist retaliated by rearranging my cutlery drawer into a family tree; my salad forks now insist they’re descended from a teaspoon that once stirred Napoleon’s coffee. I eat with my hands to avoid taking sides.

I keep a jar on the windowsill labeled “Free Time” but the lid is welded shut. Inside, moths made of unspent minutes batter themselves against the glass, leaving tiny piles of second-dust. At dusk the jar hums like a microwave full of regrets. I tell myself I’ll unscrew it on my deathbed, release the swarm so it can collapse into the shape of a life. Really I’ll probably forget it exists, and the cleaning crew will pawn it for cigarette money. They’ll smoke my abandoned afternoons, exhale perfect circles that drift out the window and accrue like halos over the traffic lights. Commuters will pause, feel mysteriously forgiven for crimes they haven’t committed yet, accelerate into the intersection with something almost like mercy.

Sometimes I scroll backwards through my camera roll until I reach the last picture of my old cat, the one where she’s mid-yawn and looks like she’s screaming the secret name of the universe. I zoom in until her pupils become black oceans where I once tried to drown my capacity for betrayal. She died on a Tuesday that tasted of pennies and overheated plastic. The vet said “she’s just a cat” and I wanted to burn the building down, but instead I went home and alphabetized every object I own: Aspirin, Ballpoint, Cremation Certificate, Doubt, Expired coupons for immortality. The cat’s ashes sit in a tin shaped like a pineapple because irony outlives us all. Occasionally I shake it like a maraca and pretend she’s still purring, a small motorized miracle requiring double-A batteries I keep forgetting to buy.

Yesterday the elevator offered me a prophecy in exchange for not pressing the alarm button. I accepted. The voice—somewhere between Siri and the girl I kissed behind the bleachers in 1998—whispered: “One day your reflection will refuse to return. You will shave a stranger’s face.” I spent the rest of the ride trying to decide whether this was a curse or a promotion. When the doors opened on my floor, the hallway stretched like taffy, each apartment door adorned with a wreath made of takeout menus. I walked past versions of myself standing in doorways holding different apologies: a bouquet of burnt-out lightbulbs, a cake frosted with eviction notices, a tiny coffin carved from the wood of the tree we once planted over our hamster. None of them met my eyes. They were waiting for someone braver, or at least more recently laundered.

At night I listen to the city’s heartbeat through the radiator. It sounds like a bank vault full of butterflies trying to unionize. Occasionally a pipe clangs Morse code: remember remember remember. I tap back: I’m trying I’m trying I’m trying. Steam answers with a sigh that smells like the inside of old hymnals. We negotiate a truce: I won’t complain about the heat if the heat doesn’t complain about my cold. Somewhere a siren Dopplers past, a mechanical wolf mourning its own velocity. I count the seconds until silence reconstitutes itself, thick as cornstarch, lumpy with the unpoured future. In that hush I almost hear the matchbox heart spark, the laugh unfurl like a stubborn dandelion breaching a sidewalk crack. Almost. Instead the radiator ticks once, twice, a metallic lullaby calibrated for tenants who pay rent in unsent letters. I close my eyes and become the third kind of silence: the one that hasn’t decided whether it’s a womb or a throat.
